answer:
Okay, let's tackle part (i) first. The question is asking to show that Marco might need to reserve at least n different friends. Wait, actually, the problem says "at least n different tables." So, we need to show that in the worst case, Marco could need n tables to ensure that no two friends who form a complete pair (i.e., their combined collections have all stickers) are seated at the same table.

First, let's understand the problem. There are n stickers to collect. Each friend has some subset of these stickers. A pair of friends is "complete" if together they have all n stickers. Marco wants to invite all his friends to a restaurant but doesn't want any complete pair to be at the same table. So, he needs to assign each friend to a table such that no two friends who form a complete pair are on the same table. The question is to show that in the worst case, he might need n tables.

To show that at least n tables are necessary, we need to construct a scenario where each friend is missing exactly one unique sticker, and thus, any two friends together would have all stickers. In this case, every pair of friends is a complete pair, so each friend A and friend B can't be at the same table, friend A and friend C can't be at the same table, etc. This would require each friend to be at a different table, hence n tables.

Let me formalize this. Suppose each friend is missing exactly one different sticker. So, friend 1 is missing sticker 1, friend 2 is missing sticker 2, ..., friend n is missing sticker n. Then, any two friends together would have all stickers because each is missing a different one. Therefore, every pair is a different sticker. So, if each friend has a different sticker, then any two friends together will have two different stickers, but not all n stickers. Wait, no. If there are n stickers, and each friend has one unique sticker, then any two friends together would have two stickers, but not all n. So, in that case, no pair is complete. But the problem says that no one has yet completed their collection. So, each friend is missing at least one sticker. But the problem says that no one has completed their collection. So, each friend is missing at least one sticker. But the problem says that a pair is complete if their joint collection contains at least one of each sticker. So, if two friends together have all n stickers, then they form a complete pair.

So, the problem is about ensuring that no two friends who together have all n stickers are seated at the same table. So, Marco needs to assign friends to tables such that no two friends who together have all stickers are in the same table. The question is to show that in the worst case, he might need n tables, and that n tables are sufficient.

For part (i), to show that Marco might need at least n tables. So, we need to find a scenario where each friend is missing exactly one sticker, and each friend is missing a different sticker. For example, friend 1 has stickers 2,3,...,n (missing 1), friend 2 has stickers 1,3,...,n (missing 2), and so on. Then, any two friends together would have all stickers. For example, friend 1 and friend 2 together have all stickers except maybe one? Wait, friend 1 has 2,3,...,n (missing 1), friend 2 has 1,3,...,n (missing 2). So together, they have 1,2,3,...,n. So they form a complete pair. Similarly, any two friends would form a complete pair. Therefore, in this case, no two friends can be at the same table. Therefore, each friend must be at a different table. Hence, Marco needs n tables. Therefore, part (i) is shown.

For part (ii), we need to show that n tables are sufficient. That is, no matter how the friends' collections are structured (as long as no one has all stickers), we can assign friends to n tables such that no two friends who form a complete pair are in the same table.

This seems like a graph coloring problem. Each friend is a vertex, and there is an edge between two friends if they form a complete pair. Then, the problem reduces to coloring the graph with n colors such that adjacent vertices have different colors. The question is to show that the graph can be colored with n colors.

But why is that the case? What's the structure of this graph?

Alternatively, maybe we can model this as a hypergraph or something else. But perhaps there's a simpler way.

Alternatively, think of each friend as a subset of the n stickers, none of which is the full set. We need to assign each subset to a table such that no two subsets whose union is the full set are in the same table.

This is similar to a graph where edges represent pairs whose union is the full set. Then, the chromatic number of this graph is at most n.

But why is that? Maybe we can find a coloring strategy.

Alternatively, think of each sticker as a color. For each sticker i, assign all friends who do not have sticker i to table i. Wait, but each friend is missing at least one sticker. So, for each friend, there is at least one sticker they don't have. So, for each friend, we can assign them to a table corresponding to one of the stickers they are missing. But if we do that, then two friends who are missing different stickers could be assigned to different tables. However, if two friends are missing the same sticker, they could be assigned to the same table. But if two friends are missing different stickers, say friend A is missing sticker i and friend B is missing sticker j (i ≠ j), then their union would have all stickers except maybe i and j? Wait, no. If friend A is missing i, they have all stickers except i. Friend B is missing j, they have all stickers except j. So together, they have all stickers except maybe i and j? No, friend A has j (since they are missing i), and friend B has i (since they are missing j). So together, they have all stickers. Therefore, if two friends are assigned to different tables based on the stickers they are missing, then if they are missing different stickers, they would form a complete pair and thus cannot be in the same table. But if we assign each friend to a table corresponding to one of the stickers they are missing, then two friends who are missing different stickers would be in different tables, which is good. However, if two friends are missing the same sticker, they could be assigned to the same table. But in that case, their union would be missing that sticker, so they don't form a complete pair. Therefore, this assignment would work.

Wait, let's think. Suppose we assign each friend to a table corresponding to one of the stickers they are missing. Since each friend is missing at least one sticker, we can choose for each friend one sticker they are missing and assign them to that table. Then, if two friends are in the same table, they are both missing the same sticker. Therefore, their union would also be missing that sticker, so they don't form a complete pair. Therefore, this assignment ensures that no two friends in the same table form a complete pair. Therefore, this would require at most n tables (since there are n stickers). However, the problem is that a friend might be missing multiple stickers. So, we need to choose for each friend one sticker they are missing and assign them to that table. But how do we ensure that this is possible? Because a friend could be missing multiple stickers, but we just need to pick one of them.

Therefore, the strategy is: for each friend, pick any sticker they are missing, and assign them to the table corresponding to that sticker. Then, in this case, any two friends in the same table are missing the same sticker, so their union is missing that sticker, hence not complete. Therefore, this assignment works, and requires at most n tables. Hence, part (ii) is shown.

But wait, the problem says "n tables will always be sufficient". So, this strategy works. Therefore, the answer for (ii) is that by assigning each friend to a table corresponding to one of the stickers they are missing, we ensure that no two friends in the same table form a complete pair. Hence, n tables are sufficient.

So, for part (i), the example where each friend is missing a unique sticker requires n tables. For part (ii), the assignment strategy using the missing stickers ensures that n tables are enough.
</think>

(i) Consider a scenario where each friend is missing exactly one unique sticker. For example, friend 1 lacks sticker 1, friend 2 lacks sticker 2, ..., friend $n$ lacks sticker $n$. Any two friends together will have all $n$ stickers (since each is missing a different one), forming a complete pair. Thus, no two friends can share a table, requiring at least $n$ tables.

(ii) Assign each friend to a table corresponding to one of the stickers they are missing. Since no friend has all stickers, each has at least one missing sticker. If two friends are in the same table, they share a missing sticker, so their union also lacks that sticker, preventing a complete pair. This uses at most $n$ tables.

\boxed{n}

instructions:
You are a math problem solver with access to a Python execution tool.

How to respond on each turn, pick exactly ONE of:

1. Call the `python_exec` tool to compute a value. The `code` argument must
   be a valid Python snippet that PRINTS the values you want to see back —
   values that are not printed are invisible to you.
   Example tool call:
     {"tool": "python_exec", "arguments": {"code": "print(sum(i for i in range(1, 101) if i % 3 == 0))"}}

2. Commit your final answer by responding with ONLY \boxed{...}, nothing
   else. Example: \boxed{42}

Never mix these two modes in a single response. Never write Python code in
a markdown fence — the only way to execute code is via the tool call.

Use the tool whenever arithmetic, enumeration, or verification would help.
When you are confident in the answer, emit the \boxed{} response.
